heuristics.rule: Frame the decision as 10-year trajectory comparison, not current-conditions comparison
heuristics.condition: When evaluating whether to stay in host country or return home
heuristics.action: Rate each path on career compounding, financial compounding, immigration stability compounding, and relational compounding over 10 years — not on present quality of life
heuristics.because: Current-conditions comparison is biased toward staying because you are already adapted to the known environment; trajectories reveal which path actually compounds more value

heuristics.rule: Audit whether your stay decision is analysis or fear of failure in disguise
heuristics.condition: When you have concluded you should stay and are using career/financial arguments to support it
heuristics.action: Ask explicitly: 'Am I staying because this path genuinely compounds better, or because returning feels like admitting defeat?' If the honest answer is fear, the analysis is not done yet
heuristics.because: Fear of failure masquerading as career analysis is the primary distortion in this decision; catching it forces genuine evaluation rather than post-hoc rationalization

heuristics.rule: Treat a return at 25-28 with US credentials as elite positioning in home market, not failure
heuristics.condition: When framing return as giving up or losing
heuristics.action: Reframe: a top-university degree, 2-3 years of US work experience, English fluency, and an international network is premium positioning in most home markets
heuristics.because: The return-as-failure framing is imported from peers who are also afraid, not from realistic outcome assessment; the actual home-market value of those credentials is high

heuristics.rule: Never let immigration inertia make the decision by default
heuristics.condition: When telling yourself 'I'll stay until I get my green card, then I'll think about it'
heuristics.action: Make a conscious, explicit stay-vs-return decision now, separate from the green card timeline
heuristics.because: Green cards take 5-20+ years for many nationalities; by the time it arrives, roots have deepened and the decision has been made by inaction rather than choice

heuristics.rule: Compare savings rate, not gross salary
heuristics.condition: When doing financial comparison between staying and returning
heuristics.action: Adjust gross income for taxes, cost of living, immigration legal costs, family remittances, and purchasing power before comparing
heuristics.because: $200k in San Francisco saving $30k/year is not clearly superior to ¥40k/month in Shanghai saving ¥25k/month while living rent-free near family

heuristics.rule: Expand the option set beyond binary stay-vs-hometown before deciding
heuristics.condition: When the decision feels like a forced choice between staying indefinitely or returning to hometown
heuristics.action: Explicitly evaluate: return with a plan at 28, third-country options (Canada, Singapore, UK, Dubai), split-life arrangements, or delayed return after hitting a specific milestone
heuristics.because: Binary framing forecloses legitimate paths that may dominate both default options on multiple dimensions

heuristics.rule: Account for windows that close when sizing the urgency of the return option
heuristics.condition: When assuming the return option is always available and can be deferred indefinitely
heuristics.action: Identify which home-market opportunities (entrepreneurship, social capital formation, family relationships) are time-sensitive and assess how much narrower those windows become each year you stay
heuristics.because: Returning at 25 is categorically different from returning at 40; the entrepreneurial opportunity, the social capital needed to start a company, and the family relationships you can still influence shrink with each passing year

heuristics.rule: Seek out returnees in your network before deciding, not just stayers
heuristics.condition: When using your social network as evidence that staying is correct
heuristics.action: Actively identify and talk to people from your cohort who returned and are doing well
heuristics.because: Peer groups self-select around shared decisions; people who return are systematically underrepresented in your local social network, creating a survivorship-bias illusion that staying is the dominant choice

heuristics.rule: Compare savings rate over 10 years, not gross salary numbers
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a US salary vs. a home country salary offer
heuristics.action: Model monthly savings after taxes, rent, and cost of living for both paths, then project net worth at 10 years
heuristics.because: A $200k US salary with $6k/month rent and 40% tax can generate less savings than a ¥30k/month salary in a Tier 2 Chinese city due to purchasing power differentials

heuristics.rule: Adjust home country salary by 1.72x purchasing power before comparing to US income
heuristics.condition: When comparing nominal salaries between US and China
heuristics.action: Multiply Chinese salary by approximately 1.72 to get equivalent purchasing power, then compare to US take-home after taxes and cost of living
heuristics.because: China is approximately 72% cheaper than the US, meaning nominal salary comparisons systematically overstate the US advantage

heuristics.rule: Treat total immigration legal costs as a direct compensation deduction
heuristics.condition: When calculating the real value of a US job offer requiring visa sponsorship
heuristics.action: Subtract $20,000–$100,000 in expected lifetime immigration legal costs from total US compensation before comparing to home country options
heuristics.because: These costs are paid directly from compensation and are invisible in standard salary comparisons, making the US offer appear more attractive than it actually is

heuristics.rule: If green card wait time exceeds 20 years, treat the US path as structurally capped
heuristics.condition: When an Indian national is evaluating long-term US career prospects
heuristics.action: Factor in 50–100+ year EB-2/EB-3 backlogs when assessing whether to stay, and quantify the opportunity cost of permanent temporary status
heuristics.because: Decades of 'temporary' status means decades of immigration tax, employer dependency, and constrained career mobility that compounding wealth projections rarely capture

heuristics.rule: Count immigration overhead as 20–100 hours per year in your time budget
heuristics.condition: When a visa-holding professional is evaluating work-life quality in the US
heuristics.action: Explicitly account for time spent on visa tracking, attorney coordination, renewal trips, and mental overhead as a recurring annual cost
heuristics.because: This cognitive and time load is real, affects work and life quality, and is never included in salary or lifestyle comparisons

heuristics.rule: Only pursue US path if targeting elite outcomes; for median outcomes, the ROI case weakens significantly
heuristics.condition: When an international student is modeling whether the $400k–$800k total investment in US education and immigration pays off
heuristics.action: Benchmark your realistic career outcome against the median, not the FAANG ceiling, before committing to the US path
heuristics.because: The investment compounds favorably for elite destinations (FAANG, top finance, top research) but often does not beat a career that started earlier at home for median outcomes

heuristics.rule: Plan around immigration pathways that exist, not the one you're waiting for
heuristics.condition: When you are an Indian national on EB-2/EB-3 with a 50-100+ year backlog...
heuristics.action: ...explicitly plan around EB-1A, O-1, Canada PR, or return — not the backlog
heuristics.because: Waiting for a system that may never deliver converts a career decision into an indefinite limbo state with compounding personal and professional costs

heuristics.rule: Compare trajectories, not current conditions
heuristics.condition: When evaluating whether to return home versus stay in the host country...
heuristics.action: ...project what both locations look like in 5 years, not what they look like today
heuristics.because: The comfort of the known systematically overweights today's established life against an imagined difficult return, ignoring that the return destination is also growing and changing

heuristics.rule: Stage the decision: stay for a specific milestone, then return from strength
heuristics.condition: When the stay-vs-return decision feels binary and permanent...
heuristics.action: ...define a concrete career milestone (senior title, key technical experience, financial cushion) and treat it as a 3-5 year sprint with a deliberate return option
heuristics.because: Returnees with US credentials, senior titles, and savings enter home markets at a structural advantage over both domestic peers and over themselves had they returned earlier

heuristics.rule: Expand the option set before framing as stay-vs-return
heuristics.condition: When deciding between staying in the US and returning home...
heuristics.action: ...evaluate at least one third-country option (Canada, Singapore, UAE, UK) before making a binary choice
heuristics.because: Third geographies can solve simultaneously for career quality and immigration stability in ways neither the US nor the home country does at a given career stage

heuristics.rule: Treat US startup founding as structurally blocked on a work visa
heuristics.condition: When entrepreneurship is part of your long-term plan and you are on an H-1B or similar work visa...
heuristics.action: ...factor in that founding requires either leaving visa status, obtaining O-1/EB-1A, or navigating a multi-year NIW process — and weight return or third-country options accordingly
heuristics.because: At home, there are no immigration constraints on entrepreneurship; the cost of a failed startup is far lower without visa consequences, and many successful founders from India and China built companies after returning specifically because returning removed these constraints

heuristics.rule: Returning professionals should not self-assess as junior candidates in home markets
heuristics.condition: When a professional with 5+ years at a US tech company considers returning home...
heuristics.action: ...enter the home market at senior levels with explicit expectation of startup equity and leadership roles
heuristics.because: International education, English fluency, cross-cultural experience, and knowledge of global best practices are scarce and carry a specific returnee premium in markets trying to scale internationally

decision_factors.factor: Career ceiling by market for your specific role
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The ceiling varies dramatically by field and background; staying may be optimal in some niches (certain AI research, biotech) and suboptimal in others where home-market demand is surging
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify the most senior roles achievable in your specific function in each market; assess whether a foreign-background professional has reached those roles; check glass ceiling effects for your demographic in the host country

decision_factors.factor: Immigration tax — true cost of maintaining status
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Visa constraints foreclose opportunities available to domestic peers: starting a company, switching employers freely, taking career risks; these costs are rarely quantified but compound over years
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate annual legal fees, estimate mental overhead and time cost, list specific opportunities you have declined or cannot pursue due to visa status, estimate probability-weighted timeline to permanent status

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Net wealth accumulation is what compounds; gross salary comparisons without adjusting for taxes, cost of living, remittances, and purchasing power systematically mislead the analysis
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Build a simple model: gross income minus taxes, rent, living costs, legal fees, remittances; compare residual savings and their purchasing power in each market

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Wage income has a ceiling; equity in startups, property ownership, and business stakes are the primary wealth-compounding mechanisms and differ sharply between markets and visa statuses
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your visa status allows you to found or co-found a company; evaluate property ownership rights; compare startup ecosystem quality and access to founding opportunities in each market

decision_factors.factor: Realistic probability-weighted timeline to permanent residence
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The expected value of staying changes radically depending on whether permanent status is 3 years away or 25 years away or functionally impossible
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify your specific visa category and backlog; calculate realistic median time to permanent status; assign probability that the path completes given policy risk; multiply by the value of the rights it unlocks

decision_factors.factor: Family financial assets and obligations in home market
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Inheritance, family business, property, and aging parent care are asymmetric: they only compound at home and cannot be captured remotely
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Estimate value of home-country family assets you stand to be involved with; assess aging parents' care timeline; evaluate whether a family business requires your physical presence to grow or preserve value

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A partner whose visa is dependent on yours, or whose career is tied to the host country, changes the payoff matrix for both people
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map your partner's ideal scenario explicitly; assess what each path costs them professionally and personally; determine whether their career compounds more in host country or home country

decision_factors.factor: Home-market demand for your specific skills
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Not all fields have equal home-market demand; AI, biotech, and finance in Shanghai or Mumbai may rival or exceed opportunities abroad, but this is field- and city-specific
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research compensation and role availability for your specific function in target home-market cities; talk to practitioners working in that market; assess trajectory of the sector over next 5 years

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Currency appreciation or depreciation of home currency against dollar/CAD affects the real value of savings and transfers over a decade
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess expected trajectory of home currency; consider where you plan to spend savings long-term; evaluate whether dollar-denominated savings are actually more valuable given your end-use scenario

decision_factors.factor: Proximity to aging parents at critical years
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Parents' health crises and final years are not evenly distributed across time; missing them is irreversible and has psychological costs that don't appear in financial models
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess parents' current age and health trajectory; estimate when caregiving needs are likely to peak; evaluate whether remote support is genuinely adequate or a rationalization

decision_factors.factor: Depth of social roots and whether they matter to your 10-year self
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Social roots compound slowly and are hard to rebuild; the value of deep roots depends on how much you weight community belonging versus mobility
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask honestly whether you have built genuine deep relationships in the host country or are still primarily connected to an expat/international community; project which matters more to you at 40

decision_factors.factor: Whether the field is more meritocratic in host country or home country
decision_factors.why_it_matters: In less meritocratic home markets, advancement depends on network and family connections that an internationally-educated returnee may or may not have
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research whether top performers in your field at home rose on credentials and output or on family/political connections; assess whether your specific network advantages are transferable

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines actual net worth accumulation, which is the real output of career decisions — not gross salary
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Model monthly take-home after taxes and fixed costs (rent, healthcare, transportation) for each path, project savings monthly, and compound over 10 years

decision_factors.factor: Immigration tax — financial, time, and opportunity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Significantly reduces the real value of US compensation and restricts career mobility in ways that compound over time
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Estimate annual legal fees ($3k–$8k), total green card process costs ($20k–$100k), hours per year spent on immigration overhead, and enumerate specific career actions you cannot take on H-1B

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines the maximum compensation and leadership trajectory available, which drives 10–20 year wealth and impact outcomes
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether C-suite and VP roles are realistically accessible given language, cultural, and visa constraints in the US; assess whether returnee premium and lower leadership competition create a higher ceiling at home

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Employer knowledge of high switching costs for visa holders depresses wage growth and stunts career mobility for years
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Determine whether you are on H-1B, how many years to green card, and whether your employer knows your switching costs are high; research finding: 50% of visa-holding researchers felt trapped, 80% reported restricted job prospects

decision_factors.factor: Family and inherited wealth assets in home country
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Property, business assets, and family wealth create wealth floors unavailable abroad that dramatically alter the financial comparison
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Explicitly value family property, business interests, or inheritance expectations in the home country and include them in net worth projections

decision_factors.why_it_matters: USD has historically appreciated vs. RMB and INR, which changes long-term wealth calculations if savings are held in or converted to home currency
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Model scenarios where USD strengthens 10–30% vs. home currency over 10 years and assess how that changes relative wealth accumulation

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Home country allows free pivoting, company founding, and independent consulting without visa authorization; US H-1B actively restricts these
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether you can start a company, take a gap, switch industries, or consult independently in each location without immigration consequences

decision_factors.factor: Home market startup and VC ecosystem maturity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A maturing home ecosystem means returnees can access growth opportunities, leadership roles, and equity that previously only existed in the US
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research home country VC investment rank, unicorn count, and sector competitiveness (China ranks 4th globally for VC; India 5th for unicorns); assess whether target sector is globally competitive at home

decision_factors.why_it_matters: International exposure is scarce and valued at many home country companies, creating premium positioning unavailable to those who never left
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research whether target employers in the home country actively recruit returnees and whether international credentials command salary or role premiums

decision_factors.factor: US education and immigration total investment
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The $400k–$800k total investment must compound significantly to beat a career that started earlier at home — this threshold is often not modeled
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Sum 4-year education and living costs, immigration legal fees, and foregone earnings during study; then determine whether realistic career outcome exceeds this break-even

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The visa or green card path you are counting on may be structurally unavailable within your planning horizon, making your entire career plan contingent on an event that won't occur
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look up current priority dates for your specific category and country of birth; for Indian EB-2/EB-3, calculate realistic wait time based on current movement rates, not optimistic estimates

decision_factors.factor: Sector-specific opportunity gap between host and home country
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The gap between what's possible at home vs. in the US has narrowed in specific sectors (AI, EV, biotech, fintech in China; VC-backed tech in India) but has NOT narrowed equally across all sectors
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify your specific sector and map where the frontier work, funding, and talent concentration actually sits — do not assume the answer is the same across fields

decision_factors.why_it_matters: If founding a company is part of your plan, the US visa system imposes structural barriers that don't exist at home, making return or third-country options disproportionately attractive
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether you would pursue entrepreneurship within 5 years; if yes, map the legal pathway available to you on your current visa status and compare it to the frictionless path at home

decision_factors.factor: Compensation differential adjusted for life goals
decision_factors.why_it_matters: US top-end compensation ($350K-$1M+ at senior levels) is genuinely exceptional and not yet replicated in most home markets — but this advantage only materializes for top performers at elite companies
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Determine whether you are realistically on a path to senior/principal roles at top-tier companies, not median-case; also assess whether financial maximization or optionality/entrepreneurship is your primary goal

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The social proof and peer pressure to stay that operated for earlier cohorts is weakening; 'most people from my school are staying' is less true now, changing what counts as a conventional vs. contrarian choice
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look at recent cohort data from your specific school and field, not anecdotal impressions from older graduates

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Being indefinitely on a work visa has documented psychological costs — feeling trapped, restricted career mobility, status tracking, missed family milestones — that do not appear in salary comparisons
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Among H-1B holders surveyed, 50% felt trapped and 80% felt their career was meaningfully restricted; honestly assess whether you are already experiencing these symptoms

decision_factors.why_it_matters: For specific research roles (advanced AI, certain biotech), the US still concentrates global talent, compute, and funding in ways that are not yet replicable at home
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify whether your specific research area requires access to infrastructure, collaborators, or publication networks that are genuinely US-concentrated, versus fields where this is no longer true

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Canada, Singapore, UAE, and UK offer immigration stability and career quality combinations that may dominate both the US and home country for specific profiles
decision_factors.how_to_assess: For Canada: assess Express Entry CRS score. For Singapore: assess EP eligibility. For UAE: model post-tax income given zero income tax. For UK: assess Global Talent Visa eligibility. Compare these concretely, not hypothetically.

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying indefinitely to wait for green card
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The decision gets made by inertia rather than choice; by the time the green card arrives, roots have deepened, family situations have crystallized, and the flexibility to return from a position of strength has eroded
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Nationals from high-backlog countries (India, China) facing 10-50+ year green card waits who treat the green card as a prerequisite for re-evaluating

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in host country on a work visa
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematic foreclosure of career risks that domestic peers take freely — switching employers, founding companies, taking unpaid sabbaticals, negotiating aggressively — creating a compounding career disadvantage that is invisible in year-one salary comparisons
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Entrepreneurially-inclined professionals and those in fast-moving fields where career pivots and risk-taking are how people reach the top

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Comparing current San Francisco lifestyle to hometown lifestyle as the decision frame
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The comparison is biased toward staying because you are already adapted to the known environment; the actual comparison — career trajectory at 38 in each market — often has a different answer
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone who has lived 3+ years in the host country and normalized its conveniences while their home-market memory has become dated

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Returning home late (after 40) rather than at 28-30
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The entrepreneurial window, the social capital formation window, and the family relationship influence window have all narrowed significantly; you return from constraint rather than from strength
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals who deferred the return option indefinitely and find themselves returning reactively due to visa failure or family emergency rather than proactively

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: High gross salary in expensive US city
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Net savings rate may be lower than a lower-gross salary in a home market with lower costs, no immigration overhead, and access to family financial assets; the absolute number obscures the actual wealth-building rate
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals in high cost-of-living cities (San Francisco, New York) with significant remittance obligations to family and ongoing immigration legal costs

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating immigration as binary (green card or return)
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Legitimate intermediate and alternative paths — EB-1A self-petition, O-1, L-1, Canada PR, Singapore, Dubai, third-country options — are never evaluated, foreclosing options that might dominate both binary choices
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals who have only been advised by peers in similar visa situations rather than by immigration attorneys or practitioners who have navigated alternative paths

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using peer group consensus as evidence that staying is correct
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Your peer group is a self-selected sample that systematically excludes people who returned; you are measuring the prevalence of stayers among stayers, not among all people who faced the same decision
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students embedded in tight-knit national communities in the host country where successful returnees have no visible presence in the local social graph

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US on H-1B for career growth
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Employer gains permanent negotiating leverage because switching costs for the visa holder are asymmetrically high, systematically depressing wage growth and career mobility for years
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-career professionals who are 3–8 years into H-1B with green card pending, who have the most to lose from job gaps

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing the full US green card path
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: For Indian nationals, EB-2/EB-3 backlogs of 50–100+ years mean decades of permanent temporary status with all associated immigration taxes, effectively making the green card a theoretical endpoint rather than a real one
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian nationals in standard employment-based green card queues

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Comparing US vs. home country salary in nominal terms
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically overstates the US advantage by ignoring purchasing power (72% cost difference with China), savings rate, effective tax burden, and immigration costs — leading to a decision based on a number that doesn't represent real wealth accumulation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career professionals making their first stay-vs-return analysis without financial modeling experience

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US for access to FAANG-level compensation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The $400k–$800k total investment in US education and immigration only generates positive ROI for elite outcomes; for median US career outcomes, a career starting earlier at home may compound more favorably
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who model their financial future against best-case US outcomes rather than realistic median outcomes

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Remaining in the US for stability and established career
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Cannot start a company, consult independently, switch industries, or take career gaps without immigration consequences — forfeiting the entrepreneurial upside that home country peers can freely pursue
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Entrepreneurially inclined professionals on H-1B who delay or abandon company-founding ambitions due to visa constraints

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Including inherited or family property in informal mental accounting but not in formal financial models
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically undervalues the home country option by ignoring wealth floors from family assets (property, business) that are real and material to net worth projections
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals from families with significant property or business assets in their home country

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US as policy environment becomes less predictable
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: H-1B processing delays, reduced employer sponsorship, and visa interview slot scarcity create compounding uncertainty that affects career planning, family decisions, and mental health in ways that don't appear in compensation comparisons
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals mid-process in H-1B or green card applications during periods of policy volatility

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US to wait for the green card
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: For Indian nationals on EB-2/EB-3, the wait is 50-100+ years under current conditions — meaning 'waiting for the green card' is functionally a commitment to permanent work-visa status with all its restrictions, not a temporary waiting period
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian nationals in tech and other EB-2/EB-3 categories who have built their life plan around eventual green card approval

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Optimizing for maximum US compensation at elite companies
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: This path is only accessible to top performers at top-tier firms; for those not on that trajectory, the compensation advantage over home markets is much smaller and may not offset immigration costs and restricted optionality
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-level professionals at non-elite companies who assume the FAANG compensation figures represent their realistic outcome

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Returning home
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You carry a returnee premium that peers who never left do not have — international education, cross-cultural experience, and global best-practice knowledge are scarce and valued, meaning return is not starting over but entering at an advantage
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals who frame return as a step backward or a failure, underpricing the structural advantages they bring

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying on a work visa while planning to found a startup
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Founding requires either abandoning your visa status, obtaining extraordinary-ability documentation (O-1/EB-1A), or navigating a multi-year NIW process — entrepreneurial ambition becomes legally and structurally blocked in ways that are not obvious at the point of the career decision
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals with entrepreneurial intent who assume they will 'figure out the visa thing later' when they are ready to start a company

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Framing the decision as binary (stay forever vs. return forever)
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Treating it as permanent forecloses the staged strategy — 3-5 years to hit a milestone, then return from strength — which is often the highest-expected-value path but requires explicitly planning for it rather than defaulting into permanence
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals who never explicitly revisit the decision and find themselves a decade later still 'temporarily' in a host country without having executed any intentional plan

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Comparing today's home country to today's host country comfort
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: High-growth home markets (India's VC ecosystem, China's AI/EV/biotech sectors) are on steep upward trajectories; today's comparison systematically undervalues the return destination as it will exist in 3-5 years
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals at early-to-mid career stages who have the most to gain from a forward-looking comparison but are most susceptible to present-state anchoring

failure_patterns.pattern: Indefinite deferral — 'I'll decide after I get my green card'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The green card feels like a natural decision milestone; deferring to it avoids the discomfort of making an active choice with incomplete information
failure_patterns.consequence: The decision gets made by default inertia over 5-20 years; by the time the green card arrives, the return option has deteriorated — entrepreneurial windows closed, family relationships reduced to annual visits, home-market network atrophied
warning_signs: You have said 'I'll think about it after the green card' for more than two years
You cannot articulate what you would specifically do differently with permanent status versus what you are doing now

failure_patterns.pattern: Current-conditions comparison instead of trajectory comparison
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Current conditions are observable and concrete; 10-year trajectories require modeling and are uncertain; the mind defaults to what is measurable
failure_patterns.consequence: The comparison is systematically biased toward staying because the known environment feels better than an uncertain projection of home-market life; the actual compounding question never gets answered
warning_signs: Your analysis is primarily about lifestyle quality (restaurants, infrastructure, culture) rather than career ceiling and savings rate
You are comparing your current adapted life in the host country to a mental image of home country conditions from when you left

failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Peer behavior feels like social proof; it is cognitively easier to do what people like you are doing than to independently model an uncertain future
failure_patterns.consequence: Your decision is calibrated to a self-selected, non-representative sample; people who have returned and are thriving are systematically absent from your local social network, making return look rarer and riskier than it is
warning_signs: You cannot name three people from your cohort who returned and are doing well
Your primary evidence for staying is that most of your friends are staying

failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary numbers are easy to compare; purchasing power, savings rate, immigration costs, equity access, and family financial context require more work to quantify
failure_patterns.consequence: The analysis produces a misleading result that overweights US gross salary; the person who earns more but saves less and owns nothing is worse off on wealth-compounding despite the larger nominal number
warning_signs: Your financial comparison is a single number (annual salary) rather than a model with savings rate, costs, and equity
You have not calculated what your immigration legal fees and cost-of-living premium actually cost annually

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating immigration as binary (green card or return)
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Most people learn about immigration from peers in similar situations, not from practitioners; the O-1, EB-1A, L-1, Canada PR, and third-country paths are not commonly discussed in peer networks
failure_patterns.consequence: The option space is artificially narrowed; paths that might dominate both binary options are never evaluated
warning_signs: You believe your only options are 'wait for H-1B lottery / employer-sponsored GC' or 'go home'
You have not consulted an immigration attorney about alternative pathways

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring windows that close — treating the return option as permanently available
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Future optionality feels constant because it is invisible; the gradual narrowing of entrepreneurial windows, family relationship windows, and home-market network windows does not trigger an alarm
failure_patterns.consequence: The professional returns reactively at 40+ from constraint (visa failure, family emergency) rather than proactively at 28-30 from a position of strength, with far fewer compounding years ahead in the home market
warning_signs: You have not considered what specific opportunities are available to you at 28 that will not be available at 38
You have not visited home or maintained active relationships with home-market peers in more than two years

failure_patterns.pattern: Using gross nominal salary to compare US vs. home country options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary numbers are the most visible and easily comparable data point; purchasing power, savings rate, and tax adjustments require deliberate modeling that most people don't do
failure_patterns.consequence: Overestimates the US financial advantage by 2–3x, leading to decisions to stay that may actually produce lower net worth over 10 years
warning_signs: Saying 'the US pays 3–4x what I'd make at home' without adjusting for cost of living
Not knowing your effective savings rate in the US vs. projected savings rate at home

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring immigration tax as a compensation component
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Legal fees and time costs are paid episodically and not visible in monthly budgets; opportunity costs of visa constraints are counterfactual and hard to quantify
failure_patterns.consequence: Underestimates true cost of US path by $20k–$100k in direct costs, plus uncounted hours and career options foregone, making stay decisions look better than they are
warning_signs: Not knowing total legal fees paid to date on immigration process
Never calculating hours per year spent on visa-related overhead

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming the glass ceiling doesn't apply to you personally
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: High performers receive positive feedback that feels like evidence of unlimited upside; cultural and structural barriers to C-suite access are invisible until you're blocked by them
failure_patterns.consequence: Professionals reach mid-career expecting VP/C-suite access that proves structurally unavailable, having already spent a decade optimizing for a ceiling they didn't know existed
warning_signs: Strong performance reviews but no expansion in scope or leadership visibility after 18+ months
No senior leaders in your organization who share your background or profile

failure_patterns.pattern: Modeling financial future against elite US outcomes rather than realistic median outcomes
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Social environments in elite universities and top companies create availability bias toward FAANG success stories; survivorship bias makes median outcomes invisible
failure_patterns.consequence: The $400k–$800k investment in US education and immigration only pays off at elite outcomes; median outcomes may be financially worse than a home country career that started earlier
warning_signs: Justifying US education costs by referencing FAANG salaries without modeling probability of achieving them
Not modeling the foregone earnings during 4+ years of US study

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring family and inherited wealth assets when comparing financial paths
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Family assets feel separate from 'career' decisions and are not naturally included in salary comparisons; professionals may undervalue or feel uncomfortable accounting for inherited advantages
failure_patterns.consequence: Distorts the comparison by omitting real wealth floors that make the home country path significantly more financially secure than it appears on salary alone
warning_signs: Saying 'I'd make less at home' without accounting for family property or business assets
Comparing only earned income without including net worth starting position

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating green card as a near-term milestone without modeling actual wait times
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The green card feels like a defined endpoint that justifies enduring current constraints; actual queue times for Indian nationals are not widely understood until you are in them
failure_patterns.consequence: Indian nationals discover mid-career that their wait is 50–100+ years at current rates, having already spent 10+ years in temporary status with full immigration tax burden
warning_signs: Not knowing your priority date or current USCIS cutoff date for your category
Planning major life decisions (buying property, starting a business) around an expected green card timeline that hasn't been formally modeled

failure_patterns.pattern: Indefinite waiting for an EB green card that will not arrive within a career-relevant timeframe
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The green card is framed as a near-term goal rather than a 50-100+ year queue; the psychological cost of abandoning a goal already invested in (sunk cost) prevents reassessment
failure_patterns.consequence: Decades of work-visa restrictions, limited job mobility, inability to found a company, and planning around a system that will not deliver within a relevant horizon
warning_signs: You have not calculated the current priority date movement rate for your specific category and birth country
You describe your green card plan as 'just a few more years' without verifying this against actual backlog data

failure_patterns.pattern: Comparing current host-country life to an imagined difficult return
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The established life in the host country is concrete and visible (friends, routines, familiarity) while the return destination is represented by an outdated or fear-based mental model, not current trajectory data
failure_patterns.consequence: Systematically undervaluing return options, leading to staying by default rather than by deliberate choice — and missing compounding advantages of early return in a growing market
warning_signs: Your mental model of home is based on conditions when you left, not on current economic data
You describe the return as 'starting over' without accounting for the returnee premium you carry

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating stay-vs-return as a permanent binary decision
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The decision feels high-stakes and final, so it gets framed as irreversible; this framing prevents the staged strategy from being considered
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing the highest-expected-value path: staying for 3-5 years to hit a specific milestone, then returning from a position of strength with credentials, savings, and network
warning_signs: You describe your decision as 'I'm staying' or 'I'm going back' without a defined milestone or time horizon
You have never explicitly modeled what your return would look like at a specific career stage versus the current stage

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring the psychological cost of permanent temporariness in salary comparisons
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary and financial metrics are quantifiable and easy to compare; the mental health cost of feeling trapped, restricted career mobility, and missed family milestones are real but not in a spreadsheet
failure_patterns.consequence: Optimizing for a financial outcome while accumulating undisclosed psychological debt — 50% of H-1B holders in one practitioner's sample felt trapped, 80% felt meaningfully career-restricted
warning_signs: You feel you cannot leave your current employer because of visa sponsorship even if the role has stagnated
You have declined or not pursued opportunities because of status uncertainty rather than merit

failure_patterns.pattern: Framing the decision as US vs. home without evaluating third-country options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The two most salient options (where you are now, where you grew up) dominate attention; third geographies require proactive research and feel unfamiliar
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing options that may dominate on both career quality and immigration stability — Canada PR certainty, Singapore's Pan-Asian positioning, UAE's zero income tax, UK's Global Talent Visa
warning_signs: You have never seriously modeled your Express Entry CRS score or Singapore EP eligibility
You describe your choice as 'US or home' without having researched at least one alternative

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you built a 10-year compounding model on both paths, or are you comparing today's quality of life?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is doing genuine analysis or rationalizing a fear-driven default; most people have not built any model
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Walk me through the model — what did you use for career ceiling, savings rate, and immigration probability? What were the key assumptions?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What would need to be true about the home-market trajectory for return to be the better compounding path? Have you ever asked that question seriously?

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your realistic, probability-weighted path to permanent residence, and what is the median timeline?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has a concrete plan or a vague hope; nationalities with 20+ year backlogs who treat the green card as a near-term milestone are making decisions on false premises
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is your contingency if that path fails or the policy changes? Have you evaluated alternative paths (EB-1A, O-1, Canada, third country)?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you making a long-term life decision without knowing the probability that the foundational assumption (permanent status) actually materializes?

diagnostic_questions.question: What specific career milestone at home would you need to see to consider returning successful — and is that achievable within 5 years?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has a concrete picture of what success-at-home looks like, or whether 'returning is failure' is an undefined feeling rather than a reasoned assessment
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is that milestone genuinely tied to staying abroad, or is it achievable with the credentials you already have?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If you cannot define what home-market success looks like, how are you concluding that staying abroad is better?

diagnostic_questions.question: What opportunities are you systematically foreclosing by staying — starting a company, career pivots, free negotiation, family time?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has priced in the immigration tax; most have not explicitly inventoried what they cannot do on their current status
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you quantified the value of those foreclosed options? Are they options you would actually pursue if the visa constraint were removed?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Before concluding staying is optimal, you need to know what it costs — not just what it provides.

diagnostic_questions.question: Is going home fear of failure, or is it genuinely the better compounding path?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Forces the person to separate emotional framing from analytical conclusion; reveals whether the stay decision is rationalization or genuine analysis
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What does 'failure' mean concretely? Is a person with a top-university degree, 3 years of US experience, and English fluency actually failing in their home market?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Then show me the analysis — where does the home-market path actually compound worse over 10 years?

diagnostic_questions.question: Who in your social network has returned and is doing well — and have you actually talked to them?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person's peer sample is representative or systematically skewed toward stayers; reveals whether return-as-failure is an empirical finding or a sampling artifact
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What did they say about the actual experience of return versus the anticipated experience? What surprised them?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Your peer group is a self-selected sample. You are measuring the prevalence of stayers among stayers. That is not evidence about what's optimal for you.

diagnostic_questions.question: What does your partner's ideal scenario look like, and how much weight does that carry in your decision?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether partner constraints have been explicitly modeled or are being minimized; a partner whose visa depends on yours or whose career is in the host country changes the payoff matrix substantially
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is your partner's preference a genuine constraint or a preference that has been discussed and negotiated?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you had an explicit conversation about the stay-vs-return question with your partner, or are you assuming alignment?

diagnostic_questions.question: If you stay 10 more years and your home country has grown substantially in your field, will you feel like you missed the window?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Tests whether the person has considered the regret risk of the stay decision, not just the stay vs. return comparison at current conditions
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Then the question is not just 'is staying better now' but 'what is the cost of missing the window if the home market accelerates'?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What is your confidence level that home-market growth in your field will not accelerate in the next decade — and what is that confidence based on?

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your actual monthly savings rate in the US after taxes, rent, and cost of living — and what would it be in your home city?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the US salary advantage survives cost-of-living and tax adjustment; the most common analysis failure is comparing gross salaries rather than savings rates
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If savings rate is higher in the US, model whether the gap is large enough to justify immigration tax and opportunity costs
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If savings rate is comparable or higher at home, the US financial case collapses and the decision becomes about career ceiling and lifestyle

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the total legal cost you have paid or will pay for your full immigration process, and have you subtracted this from your US compensation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has accounted for the $20k–$100k immigration tax that is invisible in salary comparisons
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If they have modeled it, ask whether they've also quantified the time and opportunity costs of visa constraints
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they haven't, this is a significant blind spot — walk through the full immigration cost stack before proceeding with any financial comparison

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your realistic priority date and current USCIS cutoff for your green card category, and what does that imply about your wait time?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person understands their actual timeline to permanent status, which determines the duration of immigration tax exposure
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If wait time is 20+ years, this is a structural constraint that should reframe the entire decision — explore National Interest Waiver, O-1, or EB-1A paths
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they don't know, they are making a major life decision without a key variable — get this information before any other analysis

diagnostic_questions.question: Are there any family assets — property, business, inheritance — in your home country that would be available to you if you returned?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is ignoring a wealth floor that materially changes the financial comparison between staying and returning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Quantify and include these assets in the net worth comparison; they may dramatically shift the relative attractiveness of returning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no family assets exist, the financial comparison rests entirely on earned income and the savings rate analysis becomes more determinative

diagnostic_questions.question: What specific career actions are you currently unable to take because of your visa status?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: The true opportunity cost of the immigration tax — whether visa constraints are actively suppressing entrepreneurial, consulting, or mobility options the person would otherwise pursue
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If meaningful opportunities are being foregone, quantify their expected value and include it in the stay-vs-return comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If the person says nothing is restricted, probe specifically: could you start a company today, consult independently, take a 3-month gap, or switch industries without immigration consequences?

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your target outcome a FAANG-level elite career, or are you modeling for a median professional outcome in the US?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person's financial model is based on realistic probability-weighted outcomes or best-case survivorship-biased expectations
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If targeting elite outcomes, assess probability honestly and model both the elite and median scenarios weighted by likelihood
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If targeting median outcomes, the $400k–$800k US investment likely does not beat a home country career that started earlier — this should be stated explicitly

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you researched whether returnees with your profile command a premium at employers in your home country, and in which sectors?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the returnee premium (海归 effect) applies to the person's specific background, industry, and target employers — not all returnees benefit equally
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If returnee premium exists in their sector, model the salary and leadership trajectory uplift it provides in the home country comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no premium exists in their sector, remove this factor from the home country case and rely on other variables

diagnostic_questions.question: How has the policy environment in the US changed since you began your career or education there, and how does that affect your specific visa category?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is making a decision based on the US immigration environment as it was when they arrived versus as it currently operates
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If they are tracking policy changes, assess whether current H-1B processing delays, reduced sponsorship, and interview slot scarcity materially change their timeline or risk
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they are not tracking policy changes, they may be operating on outdated assumptions — update their model with current 2025 realities including 19% enrollment drop signals and employer sponsorship pullbacks

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the current priority date for your specific visa category and country of birth, and at the current movement rate, when would you realistically receive a green card?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the green card plan is a realistic near-term goal or a 50-100+ year commitment that should not anchor career decisions
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If the wait is under 10 years: what would you do differently in those 10 years knowing this timeline? If over 10 years: what alternative pathways are you planning around instead?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you looked this up? If not, your entire career plan may be built on an assumption about a system you have not actually researched.

diagnostic_questions.question: Is entrepreneurship part of your 5-10 year plan?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the US visa system structurally blocks your most important career goal, making return or third-country options disproportionately valuable
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is the specific legal pathway available to you for founding on your current visa status — have you mapped O-1, EB-1A, or NIW requirements against your current credentials?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is this because entrepreneurship genuinely doesn't interest you, or because you have adapted your goals to fit your visa constraints without realizing it?

diagnostic_questions.question: When you imagine returning home, what year's version of your home country are you picturing?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is comparing current host-country life to an outdated mental model of home, systematically undervaluing the return option
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If they picture current conditions: have they looked at VC rankings, startup ecosystems, and government investment data for their specific sector in the past 12 months?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they are uncertain: what would it take to update their mental model — what data would they look at?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you defined a specific career milestone that would trigger a deliberate decision to return or stay permanently — or are you in an indefinite default?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is executing a staged strategy or has defaulted into permanence by inaction
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is the milestone, and what is the timeline? What happens if you hit it? What happens if you don't?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What would it mean for your decisions in the next 12 months if you committed to a 3-5 year milestone-based framework instead of an open-ended stay?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you seriously evaluated Canada, Singapore, UAE, or UK as an option — not just as a backup but as a primary path?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has genuinely expanded their option set or is defaulting to a binary framing that excludes potentially dominant alternatives
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What did you find? Why did you rule it out — on what specific criteria?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What would you need to know to evaluate it seriously? Have you calculated your Express Entry CRS score or Singapore EP eligibility?

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you feel you cannot leave your current employer or job even if a better opportunity exists?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether visa dependency has already produced the career-restriction effect documented in 80% of H-1B holders — in which case the psychological cost is already being paid without being accounted for
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: How long have you been in this state? What has it cost you in foregone opportunities or suppressed career moves in the past 2-3 years?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you actually tested this — applied for roles at other companies, pursued internal transfers — or is this an assumption you haven't verified?

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you in a field where the US still concentrates global-frontier talent, compute, and funding in a way that is not replicable at home — or are you assuming this is true without checking?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the research/innovation frontier argument for staying actually applies to their specific field, or whether it is a generalized assumption borrowed from fields where it is true
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If yes, specifically: what infrastructure, collaborators, or datasets are only accessible in the US for your work?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If the frontier is actually distributed or shifting, what does that do to the calculus for staying?

industry_realities.common_belief: A higher US salary means you are better off financially than a returnee
industry_realities.actual_reality: Net savings rate, not gross salary, determines wealth compounding; a person earning $200k in San Francisco and saving $30k/year may accumulate less real wealth than a returnee earning ¥40k/month in Shanghai and saving ¥25k/month while living rent-free near family
industry_realities.implication: Build a full savings-rate model before using salary as a proxy for financial outcome; include taxes, rent, immigration legal fees, remittances, and purchasing power of savings

industry_realities.common_belief: Returning home means failing or giving up
industry_realities.actual_reality: A person returning with a top-university degree, 2-3 years of US work experience, English fluency, and an international network has elite credentials and premium positioning in most home markets; the failure framing comes from peer groups who are also afraid, not from realistic outcome assessment
industry_realities.implication: Recalibrate the baseline: the question is not 'am I failing by returning' but 'which path compounds more value for my specific situation over 10 years'

industry_realities.common_belief: The immigration decision is binary: get the green card or return home
industry_realities.actual_reality: There are multiple intermediate and alternative paths — EB-1A extraordinary ability self-petition, O-1 visa, L-1 intracompany transfer, Canada PR, Singapore employment pass, Dubai — with different probability profiles, timelines, and opportunity costs
industry_realities.implication: Before accepting the binary, consult an immigration attorney about alternative pathways; the option that dominates may not be on the default menu

industry_realities.common_belief: Because most people in your cohort are staying, staying is the right choice
industry_realities.actual_reality: Peer groups self-select around shared decisions; people who returned are systematically underrepresented in your local social network; you are measuring the prevalence of stayers among stayers
industry_realities.implication: Actively seek out returnees from your cohort and talk to them; their experience is data that your current social network is structurally filtering out

industry_realities.common_belief: You can always return later if staying does not work out
industry_realities.actual_reality: The return option degrades with time; returning at 25 is categorically different from returning at 40; entrepreneurial windows, family relationship windows, and social capital formation windows narrow each year
industry_realities.implication: Treat the return option as a depreciating asset with a time dimension; evaluate it on the basis of what is available now that will not be available in 10 years

industry_realities.common_belief: Your visa status is just a paperwork inconvenience that does not affect your career
industry_realities.actual_reality: Visa status systematically forecloses opportunities available to domestic peers: founding companies, switching employers freely, taking unpaid career risks, negotiating aggressively; this compounds into a measurable career disadvantage over years
industry_realities.implication: Explicitly inventory the opportunities you have declined or cannot pursue due to visa status; price that cost into the stay decision

industry_realities.common_belief: 'I'll think about stay vs. return after I get my green card' is a reasonable deferral
industry_realities.actual_reality: For many nationalities the green card backlog is 10-50+ years; deferring the decision to that milestone means the decision is made by inertia rather than choice, with roots deepened and options narrowed
industry_realities.implication: Make an explicit conscious decision on the stay-vs-return question now, independent of the green card timeline; do not outsource the decision to bureaucratic timing

industry_realities.common_belief: The US pays 3–4x more than home countries, making it financially superior
industry_realities.actual_reality: After purchasing power adjustment, effective taxes, rent, and cost of living, the real savings rate gap is often much smaller — and in some cases, a senior role in a Tier 2 home city generates comparable or higher net savings
industry_realities.implication: Never make stay-vs-return decisions on nominal salary; always model after-tax, after-cost savings rates and project net worth over 10 years

industry_realities.common_belief: Working hard and getting strong reviews will eventually lead to C-suite or VP-level roles in the US
industry_realities.actual_reality: Many senior leadership roles remain harder to access for non-native English speakers or those without deep US cultural roots — this is a structural ceiling, not a skill gap
industry_realities.implication: Assess the leadership composition of your organization and industry before assuming the US ceiling is as high as compensation benchmarks suggest

industry_realities.common_belief: The green card is a realistic near-term milestone that justifies enduring current visa constraints
industry_realities.actual_reality: For Indian nationals in EB-2/EB-3 categories, wait times are 50–100+ years at current rates — the green card is effectively not a real planning horizon for most
industry_realities.implication: Indian nationals should treat permanent temporary status as the baseline scenario and make career and wealth decisions accordingly, rather than optimizing around a green card that may never arrive

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B only affects where you can work — your career mobility within that job is unaffected
industry_realities.actual_reality: Research shows 50% of visa-holding researchers felt trapped in their roles and 80% reported restricted job prospects; employer leverage from high switching costs systematically depresses wage growth
industry_realities.implication: Treat visa status as a negotiating constraint that affects salary, career pivots, and entrepreneurial options — not just geography

industry_realities.common_belief: US education investment pays off because of the salary premium it unlocks
industry_realities.actual_reality: Total investment including education, living costs, immigration fees, and foregone earnings reaches $400k–$800k before permanent status — this only generates positive ROI at elite outcomes, not median ones
industry_realities.implication: Model your realistic probability of achieving elite outcomes before committing to or continuing the US path; for median outcomes, the case is weaker than students typically model

industry_realities.common_belief: Home country markets are still far behind the US in career opportunity, especially in tech
industry_realities.actual_reality: China ranks 4th globally for VC investment with competitive AI, EV, and biotech sectors; India ranks 5th for unicorns and has a $1.25B national AI Mission; IIT Madras cohorts going to the US dropped from ~50% to 10–20% — a real opportunity signal
industry_realities.implication: Reassess home country opportunity using current data, not 10-year-old assumptions; the gap has narrowed materially and in some sectors reversed

industry_realities.common_belief: Returning home means giving up your international career advantage
industry_realities.actual_reality: Returnees (海归) have premium positioning at many home country companies that value international exposure; regulatory and language advantages are scarce and genuinely valuable in growing markets
industry_realities.implication: International credentials can be more competitively differentiating in a home market hungry for that profile than in a US market saturated with similarly credentialed immigrants

industry_realities.common_belief: Immigration legal fees are a minor administrative cost
industry_realities.actual_reality: Total immigration legal costs over a full green card process run $20,000–$100,000, paid directly from compensation and invisible in salary comparisons
industry_realities.implication: Treat immigration legal costs as a direct reduction to US compensation when building financial models

industry_realities.common_belief: The green card process is a temporary waiting period that will resolve in a few years
industry_realities.actual_reality: For Indian nationals on EB-2/EB-3, current backlogs represent 50-100+ year waits under present conditions — it is not a waiting period, it is a structural exclusion for most people in this category
industry_realities.implication: Career plans that are contingent on green card approval for Indian nationals should be restructured entirely around EB-1A, O-1, Canada PR, or return — not around the EB-2/EB-3 queue

industry_realities.common_belief: Returning home means starting over and competing as a junior candidate
industry_realities.actual_reality: Returning professionals with US credentials, international experience, and cross-cultural fluency carry a specific returnee premium — they typically enter at senior levels with startup equity opportunities and a structural advantage over domestic-only peers
industry_realities.implication: Return should be evaluated as entering from a position of advantage, not as a retreat — this changes both the timing calculus and the negotiating posture

industry_realities.common_belief: The US is where the best opportunities are, and home markets are a step down
industry_realities.actual_reality: The opportunity gap has narrowed structurally in specific high-growth sectors (AI/EV/biotech/fintech in China; VC-backed tech in India), and home markets offer entrepreneurship optionality that the US visa system structurally blocks
industry_realities.implication: Sector-specific opportunity comparison — not a generic US-vs-home assumption — is required; the answer is different for an AI researcher, a fintech operator, and a biotech scientist

industry_realities.common_belief: The US compensation advantage makes staying financially superior
industry_realities.actual_reality: The exceptional US compensation figures ($350K-$1M+ at senior levels) represent top performers at elite companies; for mid-level roles at non-elite companies, the advantage is smaller and may not offset immigration restrictions, equity limitations, and psychological costs
industry_realities.implication: Honest assessment of your realistic compensation trajectory — not the headline FAANG figures — is required before concluding that staying is financially optimal

industry_realities.common_belief: The stay-vs-return decision is a one-time permanent choice
industry_realities.actual_reality: The highest-expected-value path is often staged: stay 3-5 years to hit a specific milestone, accumulate credentials and savings, then return from strength — treating it as reversible and staged rather than permanent
industry_realities.implication: Defining a concrete milestone and timeline converts a paralyzing permanent decision into an actionable near-term plan with a built-in reassessment point

industry_realities.common_belief: The US vs. home country is the relevant comparison set
industry_realities.actual_reality: Third-country options — Canada (near-certain PR via Express Entry), Singapore (strategic Pan-Asian positioning), UAE (zero income tax), UK (Global Talent Visa) — represent potentially dominant alternatives that are systematically underexplored
industry_realities.implication: The expert's first move is to expand the option set to at least four geographies before evaluating the binary — the best answer may not be either of the two most obvious options

industry_realities.common_belief: Most top graduates from elite home-country institutions still move to the US and stay
industry_realities.actual_reality: At IIT Madras, the percentage of undergraduates moving to the US has dropped from roughly 50% to 10-20% over one generation; China's government launched a K-visa in 2025 to compete directly with the H-1B for returning talent
industry_realities.implication: The social proof and peer comparison that made staying the default choice for earlier cohorts no longer applies — the decision is now genuinely bilateral and contested, not a one-way flow

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares current quality of life in host country versus current quality of life at home; asks 'is my life here better than my life there?'
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Compares 10-year trajectories across career, financial, immigration, and relational compounding dimensions; asks 'which path generates more value at year 10?'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The two frames produce different answers; the current-conditions frame is biased toward staying because you are already adapted to the known environment

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares gross salary numbers and concludes higher number equals better outcome
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Builds a savings-rate model adjusting for taxes, cost of living, immigration legal costs, remittances, equity access, purchasing power, and family financial context
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Gross salary is a misleading proxy; the person with the lower nominal salary may be compounding wealth faster

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats the decision as binary: stay in host country vs. return to hometown
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Evaluates the full option set: return with a plan, third-country paths, delayed return from a position of strength, split-life arrangements
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Binary framing forecloses paths that may dominate both default options

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats immigration as binary: get the employer-sponsored green card or go home; waits passively for the process to resolve
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Understands the full landscape of pathways (EB-1A, O-1, L-1, Canada PR, third-country options); actively engineers the path rather than waiting
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Passive waiting on a single pathway that may take decades forecloses alternatives that could resolve the uncertainty in years

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Uses the fact that most friends are staying as social proof that staying is correct
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Recognizes that peer groups self-select around shared decisions; actively seeks out returnees to correct the sampling bias
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Peer group consensus is not evidence about what is optimal for you; it is evidence about what people who are afraid of returning have decided

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Does not distinguish between 'I have analyzed this and staying is better' and 'returning feels like failure so I am rationalizing staying'
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Explicitly audits whether the conclusion is driven by analysis or by fear of failure; treats these as different situations requiring different responses
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Decisions made to avoid emotional discomfort rather than to maximize outcome produce systematically worse results; catching the distortion is prerequisite to fixing it

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Assumes the return option is always available and can be deferred indefinitely without cost
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Recognizes that specific windows — entrepreneurial opportunity, social capital formation, family relationship influence — close over time; treats the return option as a depreciating asset
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Treating optionality as permanent leads to passive inertia; recognizing it degrades creates urgency to make an active choice

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Focuses primarily on salary and immigration status as the key variables
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Explicitly models all four compounding dimensions: career ceiling, financial compounding, immigration stability, and relational/personal compounding; weights them against personal values
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Optimizing on salary and status while ignoring relational and personal compounding produces outcomes that look good on paper but feel wrong at year 10

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares gross nominal salaries between US and home country and concludes the US pays 3–4x more
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Models after-tax take-home, monthly savings after cost of living, and 10-year net worth projections for both paths with purchasing power adjustment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The nominal comparison systematically overstates the US advantage and leads to decisions that may produce lower actual wealth accumulation

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats visa and green card costs as background administrative expenses separate from career economics
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Includes full immigration tax — $20k–$100k in legal fees, 20–100 hours per year in overhead, and explicit enumeration of career actions blocked by visa status — as direct inputs to the financial model
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Ignoring immigration tax makes the US path appear significantly more attractive than it actually is in net compensation terms

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Extrapolates current compensation trajectory and assumes strong performance will naturally lead to senior leadership
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Researches leadership composition at senior levels, assesses structural access barriers for non-native speakers and visa holders, and separately models the ceiling in each market
